caveats: a: The occupation mix is a broader-subsector proxy.
a: AI-enabled robotics and conventional automation overlap in vision-guided welding and inspection, making the substitution boundary uncertain.
rho: The cited AI adoption evidence covers manufacturing generally, not heavy-gauge tank shops.
rho: LMM firms may have poorer data and less integration talent than survey respondents.
e: The frozen LMM firm count is a margin bridge rather than a verified target list.
e: The NAICS product breadth includes standardized cylinders as well as highly customized field-erected vessels.
s5: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed deals.
s5: Some family or employee transfers may not be externally actionable acquisitions.
q: Value capture differs materially between standard factory tanks, custom pressure vessels, field erection, and maintenance.
q: Matrix is a public EPC-scale proxy with lower reported segment margins than many niche manufacturers may have.
d5: Public-company revenue, orders, and backlog are nominal and affected by price, mix, project timing, and geography.
d5: The lens includes a wider end-market mix than either proxy company.
o: Standard tank designs may consolidate into larger automated factories or shift to imports.
o: Material substitution could displace some metal vessels without eliminating the underlying storage need.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Configured or custom equipment is sold under quoted purchase orders and project contracts to industrial, energy, chemical, water, agricultural, and infrastructure customers; repeat revenue can include replacement vessels, expansions, field erection, repairs, and related fabrication.
lens.excluded_activities: Captive internal fabrication shops, shells, non-control financing vehicles, firms outside the EBITDA band, pure distributors, tank lessors without qualifying manufacturing operations, and installers that do not manufacture tanks.
lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market independent manufacturers supplying heavy-gauge metal storage tanks, pressure tanks and vessels, gas cylinders, custom-fabricated and field-erected tanks, and related repeat contract fabrication or field services to external customers.

a.bridge: BLS reports occupations for the broader NAICS 332 fabricated-metals subsector, not wage-weighted task exposure in 332420. I assign low AI substitutability to its prominent welding, machining, pressing, assembly, and supervisory work and higher exposure to estimating, engineering-document retrieval, scheduling, procurement, quality records, and administration. The broader proxy may overstate exposure where heavy-gauge and field work is especially physical and code constrained.
caveats: The occupation mix is a broader-subsector proxy.
AI-enabled robotics and conventional automation overlap in vision-guided welding and inspection, making the substitution boundary uncertain.
a.change_evidence: A six-digit occupational staffing table joined to task-level wages, plus shop-floor time studies from heavy-tank fabricators separating engineering, indirect, direct, rework, and field labor, would materially refine this share.
a.rationale: Cutting, rolling, fitting, welding, coating, pressure testing, erection, and inspection dominate the physical product. AI can reduce labor in quoting, drawing review, nesting and planning support, procurement, progress reporting, and compliance documentation, but not most direct fabrication hours.

d5.bridge: Matrix Service and Chart Industries are large, diversified public suppliers rather than US LMM 332420 firms. Matrix reported 20% higher six-month Storage and Terminal Solutions revenue driven by LNG peak shaving but lower conventional tank and terminal work; Chart reported essentially flat Cryo Tank Solutions orders and lower backlog. I translate those mixed nominal, company-specific signals into modest five-year constant-price quantity growth with a wide cyclical range. The proxies may overstate access to megaprojects or understate decentralized water, agricultural, and replacement demand.
caveats: Public-company revenue, orders, and backlog are nominal and affected by price, mix, project timing, and geography.
The lens includes a wider end-market mix than either proxy company.
d5.change_evidence: Six-digit constant-dollar shipment, booking, and backlog histories split by water, oil and gas, LNG, chemical, industrial gas, agriculture, pressure vessel, field erection, and aftermarket activity would materially move this ratio.
d5.rationale: Storage, process, water, industrial-gas, LPG, LNG, replacement, and regulatory needs provide a durable base. Oil and gas capital cycles, fewer large terminal projects, customer concentration, long award timing, and alternative vessel materials create meaningful downside.

caveats: The frozen LMM firm count is a margin bridge rather than a verified target list.
The NAICS product breadth includes standardized cylinders as well as highly customized field-erected vessels.
e.change_evidence: A verified census of the 293 dataset-estimated LMM firms covering ownership, certifications, employee depth, customer concentration, captive status, manufacturing content, and field-service mix would materially change eligibility.
e.rationale: A large share of specialist tank and vessel fabricators can be merchant, employee-based, and transferable under the default lens. Captive shops, businesses dominated by an owner's code credentials or customer relationships, very large EPC operators, weakly certified firms, and non-manufacturing rental fleets reduce eligibility.

l.detail: QCEW 2024 private total annual wages at 332420 ($2,989,436,086) x benefits multiplier 1.4294 [ECEC private-industry total comp $46.60/hr / wages $32.60/hr = 1.4294 (March 2026 reference period)] / SUSB 2022 receipts at 332420 ($11,362,778,000). Vintage mismatch: 2024 wages over 2022 receipts, stated. Level: 6-digit. Harmonized to IPS basis ÷1.3407 (median of 65 dual-method codes).

n.detail: margin 0.1962 (margins.json[33]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Machinery EBITDA/Sales 19.62%); revenue/employee $334,180 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 332420); class <5: 96 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,008,615 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $197,890 -> out of $1-10M band; class 5-9: 69 firms, avg revenue/firm $1,743,536 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $342,082 -> out of $1-10M band; class 10-19: 93 firms, avg revenue/firm $3,628,043 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $711,822 -> out of $1-10M band; class 20-99: 213 firms, avg revenue/firm $11,980,141 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $2,350,504 -> IN $1-10M band; class 100-499: 80 firms, avg revenue/firm $50,114,425 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $9,832,450 -> IN $1-10M band; class 500+: 45 firms, avg revenue/firm $94,384,778 (class receipts/firms), implied EBITDA $18,518,293 -> out of $1-10M band; n_band = 293

caveats: Standard tank designs may consolidate into larger automated factories or shift to imports.
Material substitution could displace some metal vessels without eliminating the underlying storage need.
o.change_evidence: Customer make-versus-buy studies and procurement interviews measuring insourcing, modular or composite substitution, imports, additive manufacturing, and software-enabled design-to-factory self-service would materially change this share.
o.rationale: The current basket requires physical vessels, accountable design and fabrication, traceable materials, qualified welding, testing, delivery, and often field erection. AI can alter workflow and fewer labor hours may be required, but customers cannot self-serve heavy-gauge tanks through software within five years.

caveats: Value capture differs materially between standard factory tanks, custom pressure vessels, field erection, and maintenance.
Matrix is a public EPC-scale proxy with lower reported segment margins than many niche manufacturers may have.
q.change_evidence: A representative sample of LMM bids and contracts showing fixed-price versus reimbursable terms, material escalation, open-book clauses, rebid frequency, price elasticity, and margin movement after productivity gains would materially change retention.
q.rationale: Quoted and project-based work can retain productivity gains through an award, especially where qualifications, engineering, field capability, and lead time differentiate the supplier. Competitive bidding, sophisticated customers, transparent steel inputs, change-order negotiation, and recurring rebids should pass through a substantial share over five years.

caveats: The cited AI adoption evidence covers manufacturing generally, not heavy-gauge tank shops.
LMM firms may have poorer data and less integration talent than survey respondents.
rho.change_evidence: Audited deployments at independent tank fabricators with pre/post labor hours, bid accuracy, schedule adherence, rework, weld quality, and customer or code-inspector acceptance would materially move implementation.
rho.rationale: Document-heavy estimating, production control, purchasing, and quality workflows are implementable within five years. Legacy drawings, uneven ERP data, one-off field conditions, weld procedures, traceability, customer approvals, and pressure-vessel liability require human verification and slow deployment in the most consequential work.

s5.bridge: Gallup measures stated five-year sale or transfer plans across US employer-business owners, with 22% reporting such plans. I shade the base below that rate because intentions do not always produce a qualifying completed control transfer, while retaining an upper range for older owners in specialist fabrication. The proxy can overstate completion or understate unsolicited transactions.
caveats: Gallup is cross-industry and measures plans rather than completed deals.
Some family or employee transfers may not be externally actionable acquisitions.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of completed 332420 private-company control transactions from PitchBook, GF Data, Axial, BizBuySell, and specialist industrial M&A advisers, divided by a verified eligible-firm denominator, would materially change this probability.
s5.rationale: National succession evidence supports regular potential exits, but owner dependence, bonding, certifications, working-capital needs, cyclicality, and buyer diligence can stop a planned transition from closing.

principal_driver: A broad installed base and unavoidable physical storage needs support recurring replacement, expansion, fabrication, and field work.
principal_weakness: Most labor is physical and project economics are exposed to competitive bids, materials, warranties, and volatile large awards.

caveats: The default whole-code lens spans standardized factory cylinders, custom pressure vessels, and field-erected tanks despite lens.heterogeneous being false; those activities can have materially different labor exposure, implementation friction, competitive bidding, and value capture, although the breadth is visible and does not demonstrate a different base tier or action.

S1 establishes the 2017 product and service boundary but not the current product mix, independent-firm share, repeat-revenue prevalence, transferable-target population, or operator-required share; e, q, and o remain honest estimates rather than source-observed values.

The q base of 0.52 is not measured by S1 or S6: Matrix's segment margin and warranty experience provide context on project economics, not the fraction of AI productivity retained by an LMM operator. Moving q within its disclosed range does not change the LOW_PRIORITY base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action because H remains the weakest link.

The o range of 0.92 to 0.997 is narrow and high without direct evidence on imports, insourcing, modular or composite substitution, or design-to-factory self-service; the estimate is directionally plausible for physical vessels but should be tested with the change evidence the packet identifies.

The d5 bridge converts two large public-company nominal revenue, order, and backlog snapshots into five-year constant-price quantity growth for a broader LMM end-market basket. The packet discloses this mismatch, and the low scenario still leaves D well above the factor that sets the base tier.

sources_audited.note: The Census survey instrument supports the stated 2017 industry and product breadth, including gas cylinders, pressure tanks, factory-complete storage tanks, custom-fabricated process vessels, field-erected tanks, and contract metalworking. It is a scope source, not evidence for the numeric e, q, or o estimates.

The 2025 BLS NAICS 332 page reports the cited broader-subsector employment counts for welders, assemblers, machinists, press operators, and production supervisors. The packet correctly labels its translation to six-digit task exposure as a proxy.

The July 2026 NIST roadmap supports the attributed manufacturing-AI opportunity set and the stated data, integration, trustworthiness, reliability, and safety barriers; it does not measure heavy-tank-fabricator adoption or substitution.

The NAM survey reports 18% with a manufacturing-operations AI strategy, 43% with one in progress, 65% lacking useful data, and 62% reporting data not formatted or structured for AI use. The packet appropriately treats rho as a cross-manufacturing estimate rather than an observed 332420 result.

Gallup reports that 22% of employer-business owners planned a sale or ownership transfer within five years, and separately cites Census data that 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older. The packet explicitly discloses the cross-industry plans-to-completions bridge.

Matrix's filing reports six-month Storage and Terminal Solutions revenue up 20% from LNG peak-shaving volume, partly offset by lower tank and terminal work; margin fell from 6.9% to 5.4%, and specialty-tank warranty and commercial matters reduced gross profit by $3.6 million.
Chart's filing reports 2025 Cryo Tank Solutions orders of $587.8 million versus $582.9 million and backlog of $248.0 million versus $290.3 million, supporting the attributed mixed directional signal.

sources.supports: Defines industry product breadth including gas cylinders, pressure tanks, storage tanks, refinery and process vessels, custom factory fabrication, field-erected tanks, and contract metalworking.

sources.supports: Shows the broader subsector's physical production mix, including 112,520 welders, 125,250 assemblers, 101,640 machinists, 56,060 press operators, and 67,090 production supervisors in 2025.
sources.title: Fabricated Metal Product Manufacturing: NAICS 332

sources.supports: Documents AI opportunities in manufacturing analytics, sensing, digital twins, robotics, logistics, and supply chains, and barriers involving data, integration, explainability, reliability, and safety.
sources.title: 2026 Roadmap on Artificial Intelligence and Machine Learning for Smart Manufacturing

sources.supports: Manufacturing survey reports 18% have a formal operations AI strategy, 43% are developing one, 65% lack the right data, and 62% cite unstructured or poorly formatted data.
sources.title: Shaping the AI-Powered Factory of the Future

sources.supports: Gallup reports 22% of US employer-business owners planned a sale, transfer, or public offering within five years, and 52.3% of employer businesses are owned by people age 55 or older.
sources.title: Most Small-Business Owners Lack a Succession Plan

sources.supports: Reports six-month Storage and Terminal Solutions revenue up 20% from LNG peak-shaving volume but partly offset by lower tank and terminal work; segment margin fell from 6.9% to 5.4% and specialty-tank warranty matters reduced gross profit by $3.6 million.
sources.title: Matrix Service Company Quarterly Report for the Period Ended December 31, 2025

title: Metal Tank (Heavy Gauge) Manufacturing
